error.signature: AI ignores Singapore's strict behavioral laws (chewing gum ban, littering fines, drug penalties)

verdict.summary: Singapore enforces some of the world's strictest behavioral laws. Violations that are minor elsewhere (littering, jaywalking, gum chewing) carry heavy fines or even corporal punishment. AI must warn users about these when advising on Singapore travel or business.

dead_ends.action: Suggest bringing chewing gum into Singapore
dead_ends.why_fails: Import, sale, and distribution of chewing gum is banned in Singapore since 1992. Exception only for therapeutic/dental gum from pharmacies with prescription. Fine up to SGD 100,000 or 2 years jail for trafficking.

dead_ends.action: Treat littering, jaywalking, or spitting as minor offenses in Singapore
dead_ends.why_fails: First-offense littering: SGD 2,000-10,000 fine. Repeat offenders face Corrective Work Orders (public cleaning in a vest). Jaywalking: SGD 1,000 fine or 3 months jail. Singapore enforces these rigorously.

dead_ends.action: Assume recreational drug use is tolerated in Singapore
dead_ends.why_fails: Singapore has mandatory death penalty for drug trafficking above threshold amounts (15g heroin, 500g cannabis). Even possession of small amounts leads to lengthy imprisonment and caning. Zero tolerance.
dead_ends.condition: Any drug-related advice for Singapore context

workarounds.action: Before Singapore travel advice, explicitly warn about: no chewing gum, no durian on public transport, no eating/drinking on MRT, no smoking except designated areas, no jaywalking, carry trash to bins.

For business in Singapore: punctuality is expected (influenced by British colonial heritage), business cards exchanged with both hands, dress formally despite tropical climate. English is business language.
Respect Singapore's multiethnic, multireligious society (Chinese, Malay, Indian, Eurasian). Avoid racial/religious comments — the Sedition Act criminalizes promoting hostility between racial groups.
